segments.caption: The video opens with a red-tinted title card displaying 'FRONTLINE' before transitioning to an aerial view of Moscow, showcasing St. Basil’s Cathedral and the Kremlin under a clear blue sky, with the text 'SEPTEMBER 2022' overlaid. The scene shifts to a grand, ornate hall filled with a large audience seated in rows, facing a stage adorned with Russian flags and the national coat of arms. The attendees, dressed in formal attire, are shown in close-ups, including a man in a blue military uniform with medals, a woman in a black blazer with a white blouse, and a man with gray hair and glasses. Two uniformed guards in dark blue ceremonial attire open ornate golden doors, allowing a man in a dark suit to enter and walk down a polished wooden corridor toward the stage. He is greeted with applause as he ascends the stage and takes his place at a podium. The camera focuses on him as he begins speaking, with subtitles reading: 'Western countries have been saying for centuries that they bring freedom and democracy to other nations. Nothing could be further from the truth.' The setting is opulent, with chandeliers, gilded decorations, and a formal, serious atmosphere.

segments.caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Vladimir Putin in black-and-white, his expression stern and contemplative, setting a serious tone. The scene transitions to wide aerial shots of a massive protest in a city square, with crowds waving red and orange flags, banners, and signs, including one with a crossed-out heart over 'RUSSIA PUTIN'. The crowd is dense, moving forward in unison, and many hold signs with Russian text demanding fair elections and criticizing the government. A close-up reveals a protester holding a sign with a black-and-white image of a man in a suit, labeled 'ЧЕСТНЫЕ ВЫБОРЫ' (Fair Elections). The footage cuts to Yevgenia Albats, a Russian journalist, speaking in an interview setting, wearing glasses and a red collared shirt under a black jacket. The video then returns to the protest, showing black-and-white close-ups of demonstrators shouting and raising fists, with one holding a sign reading 'ТАКИЕ «ВЫБОРЫ» — ПЛЕВОК В НАРОД!' (Such 'elections' — a spit in the face of the people!). The segment ends with chaotic nighttime footage of police in riot gear arresting protesters, with the PBS and FRONTLINE logos visible, indicating the source of the footage.

segments.caption: The video opens with chaotic street footage of a protest, where a crowd of journalists and photographers, including one in an orange shirt with a backpack, are surrounded by police in riot gear. The officers, wearing helmets and camouflage uniforms, are seen arresting individuals and pushing through the crowd, with a police bus labeled 'ПОЛИЦИЯ' (POLICE) visible in the background. On-screen text overlays describe escalating state actions: 'They started enacting searches, arrests, detentions. Actions against opposition leaders. Persecution in the mass media.' The scene transitions to an interview with Gennady Gudkov, an opposition politician, who speaks about widespread individual persecution affecting 'tens of hundreds, maybe thousands of people in the country.' The video then cuts to a montage of newspaper clippings and news headlines from outlets like the New York Times and BBC, detailing the mysterious deaths and poisonings of Kremlin critics such as Boris Nemtsov, Mikhail Lesin, and Vladimir Kara-Murza, with headlines like 'More of Kremlin’s critics are mysteriously turning up dead' and 'Poisoned critic Vladimir Kara-Murza leaves Russia for treatment.' The segment concludes with an interview with Vladimir Kara-Murza, a Russian opposition politician, who speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, his expression serious and reflective, as he discusses the dangers faced by dissenters in Russia.

segments.caption: The segment begins with a close-up of a man with a receding hairline and beard, wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, suggesting an interview or commentary setting. The scene transitions to a green-tinted satellite view interface, zooming from an aerial shot of The Kremlin in Moscow, Russia, labeled with targeting graphics and a 'SIGNAL STATUS' bar, then panning to a broader map highlighting Russia and Ukraine, with Crimea marked in green. The interface has a digital, surveillance-like aesthetic with grid lines and signal indicators. The video then cuts to footage from 2014, showing a group of soldiers in green camouflage uniforms, helmets, and balaclavas, marching in formation with rifles, some carrying equipment, moving through urban and rural environments. The soldiers are seen in various positions—marching, standing guard, and taking cover—indicating military deployment. The segment intersperses these visuals with interviews: first, Steven Pifer, identified as U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine (1998–2000), speaking in a formal setting with a dark suit and red patterned tie; then William Taylor, U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine (2006–09), also in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious expression. The soldiers are shown again in different settings—standing in a line, walking through a field, and taking defensive positions—emphasizing the military presence and tension. The overall tone is serious and documentary, focusing on geopolitical conflict and expert analysis.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a soldier in full camouflage gear, wearing a patterned balaclava and helmet, holding a rifle, with other soldiers blurred in the background, suggesting a tense military presence. The scene quickly shifts to a field where a large artillery piece fires, sending a plume of smoke into the air, followed by soldiers running through a dusty, war-torn landscape. The footage then cuts to a black-and-white sequence showing the devastating aftermath of conflict: a heavily damaged apartment building with rubble piled high, civilians navigating the wreckage, and a woman in distress, her hand to her face, standing before a wall riddled with bullet holes. The somber tone continues with another woman, visibly shaken, gesturing in anguish. The segment transitions to an interview with Eugene Robinson of The Washington Post, who speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting. The final shot is a haunting black-and-white image of multiple bodies covered in tarps, lying on the ground, with armed soldiers and civilians in the background, underscoring the human cost of war. Throughout, the visual style alternates between color and stark black-and-white, emphasizing the contrast between active combat and its tragic aftermath.

The FRONTLINE logo appears in the bottom left corner during both interviews.

segments.caption: The video opens with chaotic, handheld footage of a violent confrontation at night, showing police in riot gear arresting protesters near the U.S. Capitol, with one officer in a black jacket and helmet grappling with a man on the ground. The scene quickly shifts to daytime, wide-angle shots of the U.S. Capitol Building under siege, with a massive crowd of protesters, many wearing red hats and waving 'TRUMP 2020' flags, breaching the building’s exterior and climbing its steps and walls. Inside, the chaos continues as protesters surge through arched doorways and corridors, some holding flags and recording on phones. The footage cuts to an interview with Timothy Snyder, identified as 'Author, The Road to Unfreedom,' speaking earnestly in a dimly lit room with a blue shirt. This is followed by bodycam footage from January 6, 2021, showing police in riot gear being overwhelmed by protesters, with one officer being pushed to the ground. The video then transitions to a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, his expression stern and intense, followed by a green-tinted satellite view interface zooming from the White House to Geneva, Switzerland, with targeting graphics and signal status bars. The scene shifts to June 2021, showing Joe Biden and Vladimir Putin shaking hands on a red carpet in Geneva, flanked by U.S. and Russian flags, with Biden placing a hand on Putin’s arm as they walk into a building. The video cuts to an interview with Evan Osnos, identified as 'Author, Joe Biden,' speaking in a dark room with a suit and light blue tie. The final shot returns to Biden and Putin shaking hands, with Biden smiling and Putin maintaining a neutral expression, as they enter the building together.

The tone abruptly changes to a stark black-and-white office setting, where Joe Biden, in a dark suit and striped tie, is seated at a large ornate desk, writing intently, with an American flag and presidential seal visible. He then speaks with expressive hand gestures, flanked by an aide in a suit, while a woman in the background listens. The scene cuts to Susan Glasser, in a red blazer and patterned scarf, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting. The video then transitions to aerial combat footage: a snowy landscape with military vehicles moving through snow, followed by a massive explosion on a road with tanks and armored vehicles, and then a wide shot of a field with multiple explosions and smoke trails. A close-up black-and-white profile of Vladimir Putin, intense and focused, appears, followed by Kori Schake, Director of the American Enterprise Institute, speaking in a color interview, wearing a navy blazer with red lapels and a yellow-and-blue ribbon, identified by on-screen text. The segment ends with a return to the war-torn urban environment, showing a shattered glass view with a blurred vehicle behind, reinforcing the theme of destruction and vulnerability.

segments.caption: The segment opens with Kori Schake, Director of the American Enterprise Institute, speaking in a close-up interview shot; she wears a navy blazer with red lapels and a yellow-and-blue ribbon, indicating support for Ukraine. The scene abruptly shifts to a violent explosion in a green, wooded area, followed by a fiery blast engulfing a structure, suggesting a war zone. The footage then transitions to a serene view of the Kremlin, with its golden domes and Russian flag waving under a partly cloudy sky, contrasting the earlier chaos. A black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin follows, showing him in a suit with a stern expression. The video then cuts to a montage of news clips: a rural Ukrainian village under attack with smoke rising, labeled 'CBS Mornings | BREAKING OVERNIGHT'; protesters being arrested in a city square, with a sign reading 'ЧЕСТНЫЕ ВЫБОРЫ' (Fair Elections); and Putin seated at a desk, with ABC News reporting 'PUTIN UNDER PRESSURE' after calling up more troops. The montage continues with Putin on CNBC, with the headline 'EUROPE FACES ENERGY CRISIS AS PUTIN THREATENS TOTAL ENERGY CUT-OFF,' followed by a glitchy transition to Fox News, which declares 'PUTIN THREATENING TO USE NUCLEAR WEAPONS' as soldiers in camouflage are shown in a field. The segment ends with Putin again on Fox News, reinforcing the nuclear threat, with the same breaking news banner. The sequence juxtaposes expert commentary with real-time war footage and media coverage, emphasizing escalating geopolitical tension and the human cost of conflict.

The scene quickly transitions to Antony Blinken, Secretary of State, in a color interview, wearing a dark suit with a U.S. flag pin, speaking seriously with hand gestures in a dimly lit room with an American flag in the background. The video then cuts to a stark black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, his face illuminated dramatically against a dark backdrop, emphasizing his intense, focused gaze. This is followed by a similar black-and-white close-up of a man with a stern expression, likely a U.S. official, before shifting to another man in a suit and glasses, speaking directly to the camera in a color interview, his expression serious and engaged. The sequence uses dramatic lighting and tight framing to convey urgency and gravity, alternating between color and monochrome to heighten the tension of the geopolitical narrative.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man’s back, shown from behind as he walks forward. The man, wearing a dark suit with a white collared shirt, has a receding hairline and is silhouetted against a bright, gradient background that fades from white to dark at the edges, creating a stark, high-contrast visual. As he walks, the camera slowly zooms out, revealing more of his figure and the surrounding environment. On either side, dark curtains begin to part, revealing ornate, possibly heraldic, designs—suggesting a formal or ceremonial setting. The lighting remains focused on the man, emphasizing his silhouette and creating a sense of mystery and gravity. The scene then fades to black, transitioning into a series of funding and production credits. These appear over a dark, digital-style background with faint, blurred imagery and horizontal light streaks. The text is white and clearly legible, listing sponsors such as the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, PBS Viewers, Abrams Foundation, MacArthur Foundation, Park Foundation, Heising-Simons Foundation, and the FRONTLINE Journalism Fund. The credits continue with production details, including the title 'Putin and the Presidents,' directed by Michael Kirk and reported by Vanessa Fica. Additional credits list crew members, contributors, and sources, including archival footage from the Clinton and Obama Presidential Libraries, Getty Images, and the New York Times. The segment concludes with a URL for more information: pbs.org/frontline, displayed in the lower right corner throughout the credits.
